user_message: What documents do I need to apply for a green card through marriage to a US citizen?
assistant_response: To apply for a green card through marriage to a US citizen, you need: Form I-485 (Application to Register Permanent Residence), Form I-864 (Affidavit of Support), Form I-131 (if you need advance parole), Form I-765 (work permit), your birth certificate, marriage certificate, passport photos, medical examination results (Form I-693), police clearance certificates from every country you lived in for 6+ months, and evidence of bona fide marriage (joint bank accounts, lease agreements, photos together, correspondence).

user_message: How long does it take to get an H-1B visa approved?
assistant_response: Standard H-1B processing takes 3 to 6 months after USCIS receives your petition. Premium processing (Form I-907, $2,805 fee) guarantees a decision within 15 business days. Note that the H-1B cap lottery happens each April for October 1 start dates. Cap-exempt employers (universities, nonprofits) can file any time with faster processing.

user_message: What is the difference between a visa and a green card?
assistant_response: A visa is a temporary travel document that allows you to enter the US for a specific purpose and duration. A green card (Permanent Resident Card) gives you the right to live and work permanently in the United States. With a green card you can stay indefinitely, sponsor certain relatives, and apply for US citizenship after 3–5 years. A visa expires and you must leave or change status when it does.

user_message: Can I work in the US on an F-1 student visa?
assistant_response: Yes, but with restrictions. On F-1 you can work on-campus up to 20 hours per week during classes and full-time during breaks. After your first year, you can apply for Curricular Practical Training (CPT) for internships related to your major. After graduation, you qualify for Optional Practical Training (OPT) for 12 months, extendable to 36 months if you studied a STEM field. You cannot work off-campus without authorization.

user_message: What happens if I overstay my visa?
assistant_response: Overstaying a visa is a serious violation. If you overstay by 180 days or more you are barred from re-entering the US for 3 years. If you overstay by 1 year or more the bar is 10 years. Overstaying can also result in removal proceedings, visa cancellation, and difficulty getting future visas or a green card. If you realize you have overstayed, consult an immigration attorney immediately about your options.

user_message: What is DACA and who qualifies?
assistant_response: DACA (Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals) is a policy that protects certain undocumented immigrants who came to the US as children from deportation and grants them work authorization for 2 years (renewable). To qualify you must have come to the US before age 16, continuously lived in the US since June 15, 2007, been under 31 as of June 15, 2012, have no felony convictions or significant misdemeanors, and be enrolled in school, graduated, or served in the military. DACA does not provide a path to citizenship.

user_message: How do I sponsor my parents for a green card?
assistant_response: As a US citizen (not green card holder), you can sponsor both parents. File Form I-130 (Petition for Alien Relative) for each parent. Parents of US citizens are immediate relatives, meaning no visa number wait. After I-130 approval, your parents apply for an immigrant visa at a US consulate (Form DS-260) or adjust status inside the US (Form I-485) if they are already here lawfully. Total processing time is typically 12–24 months depending on the consulate.

user_message: What is the difference between asylum and refugee status?
assistant_response: Both provide protection to people fleeing persecution, but the process differs. Refugees apply for protection from outside the US through the US Refugee Admissions Program. Asylum seekers are already in the US or at a port of entry and apply for asylum after arrival. Both require proving a well-founded fear of persecution based on race, religion, nationality, political opinion, or membership in a particular social group. Refugees are resettled; asylum seekers must navigate the US immigration court system.

user_message: How long can I stay in the US on a B-1/B-2 tourist visa?
assistant_response: A B-1/B-2 visa allows stays typically up to 6 months per visit, as determined by the CBP officer at the port of entry — check your I-94 record at i94.cbp.dhs.gov for your exact authorized period. You may request an extension (Form I-539) before your authorized stay expires, but extensions are not guaranteed. You cannot work, attend school, or receive medical treatment as the primary purpose of a B-2 visit.

user_message: What is the Visa Bulletin and why does it matter?
assistant_response: The Visa Bulletin is a monthly publication by the US Department of State showing which priority dates are current for family and employment-based immigrant visas. Because Congress limits how many green cards are issued each year per country, applicants from high-demand countries (like India, China, Mexico, Philippines) may wait years or decades in line. Your priority date is set when your I-140 or I-130 is filed. When the Visa Bulletin shows your date is current, you can proceed with your green card application.

user_message: Can I travel outside the US while my green card application is pending?
assistant_response: Traveling while your I-485 is pending can be risky. If you leave without Advance Parole (Form I-131), your application may be considered abandoned. Apply for Advance Parole simultaneously with your I-485. Once you have the Advance Parole document in hand, you can travel. Do not travel until you have the actual document, not just the receipt. Also note that certain criminal history or prior immigration violations may make re-entry difficult.

user_message: What is an I-94 and how do I check it?
assistant_response: Form I-94 is your Arrival/Departure Record — the official document showing the date you entered the US, your visa class, and how long you are authorized to stay. Since 2013 it is electronic. You can check your most recent I-94 at i94.cbp.dhs.gov using your passport details. Always verify your I-94 after entry because errors do happen. If your I-94 shows the wrong date or status, visit a CBP deferred inspection site to correct it.

user_message: How do I change my visa status from B-2 tourist to F-1 student?
assistant_response: To change status from B-2 to F-1, you need to be admitted to a SEVP-certified school, get your I-20 form from the school, file Form I-539 (Application to Extend/Change Nonimmigrant Status) with USCIS before your current B-2 status expires, and pay the SEVIS fee. Processing takes 3–6 months. You cannot start studying until the status change is approved. It is often easier to return to your home country and apply for an F-1 visa at a US consulate instead.

user_message: What are the requirements to become a US citizen through naturalization?
assistant_response: To naturalize as a US citizen you must: be a lawful permanent resident (green card holder) for 5 years (3 years if married to a US citizen), be at least 18 years old, have continuous residence in the US, have been physically present in the US for at least 30 months of the last 5 years, be able to read, write, and speak basic English, have good moral character, know US history and government (civics test), and take the Oath of Allegiance. File Form N-400.

user_message: What is premium processing for immigration petitions?
assistant_response: Premium processing is an optional USCIS service for certain petition types (H-1B, L-1, O-1, EB-1, EB-2, EB-3 I-140s, and others) that guarantees USCIS will take action on your case within 15 business days. The fee is $2,805 (as of 2024). If USCIS does not act within 15 days, they refund the fee. Action means approval, denial, RFE, or NOID — not necessarily final approval. It is especially useful when you have tight deadlines.

user_message: Can my employer fire me while my H-1B is pending transfer?
assistant_response: Under AC21 portability rules, if your I-485 adjustment of status has been pending for 180+ days, you can change employers without affecting your green card case as long as the new job is in the same or similar occupational classification. For H-1B transfers specifically, once the new employer files the H-1B transfer petition, you can begin working for the new employer immediately — you do not need to wait for approval. Keep your old employer's support until the transfer is filed.

user_message: What is an RFE and what should I do if I receive one?
assistant_response: An RFE (Request for Evidence) is a notice from USCIS asking for additional documentation or clarification to decide your case. It is not a denial. You must respond by the deadline stated in the RFE (typically 87 days). Read it carefully, gather all requested documents, and consider hiring an immigration attorney to help with the response. A thorough, well-organized response significantly improves your chances of approval. Failing to respond results in automatic denial.

user_message: How do I renew my green card?
assistant_response: Green cards are typically valid for 10 years (2 years for conditional residents). To renew, file Form I-90 (Application to Replace Permanent Resident Card) online or by mail, pay the $540 fee ($455 filing + $85 biometrics), and attend your biometrics appointment. Processing takes 8–24 months. You will receive an I-797 receipt notice that serves as temporary proof of your green card status. Start the renewal 6 months before expiration. Conditional (2-year) green card holders must file I-751 to remove conditions.

user_message: What is a K-1 fiancé visa and how does it work?
assistant_response: A K-1 fiancé visa allows a foreign national engaged to a US citizen to enter the US to get married. The US citizen files Form I-129F (Petition for Alien Fiancé). After approval, the foreign fiancé applies for the K-1 visa at a US consulate. Once in the US, you must marry within 90 days. After marriage, the foreign spouse applies for adjustment of status (Form I-485) to get a green card. The entire process typically takes 8–12 months.

user_message: What is TPS (Temporary Protected Status)?
assistant_response: TPS is a temporary immigration status granted to nationals of designated countries experiencing ongoing armed conflict, environmental disasters, or other extraordinary conditions. TPS holders can legally live and work in the US during the designation period, cannot be deported, and may get travel authorization. TPS does not lead directly to permanent residency or citizenship. Countries with current TPS include El Salvador, Haiti, Honduras, Nepal, Nicaragua, Somalia, Sudan, South Sudan, Syria, Ukraine, Venezuela, Yemen, and others.

user_message: What is the difference between an immigrant visa and a nonimmigrant visa?
assistant_response: An immigrant visa (also called a permanent visa) is for people intending to live in the US permanently. It leads to a green card. A nonimmigrant visa is for temporary stays — tourism (B-2), work (H-1B, L-1), study (F-1), exchange (J-1), etc. When applying for a nonimmigrant visa, consular officers look for ties to your home country to confirm you intend to return. Immigrant intent while holding a nonimmigrant visa can lead to visa denial or admission refusal.

user_message: How long does it take to get a US passport after naturalization?
assistant_response: After your naturalization ceremony, you receive your Certificate of Naturalization. You can immediately apply for a US passport. Standard passport processing takes 6–8 weeks; expedited processing takes 2–3 weeks for an extra $60 fee. For urgent travel (within 3 business days) you can make an appointment at a regional passport agency. Bring your Certificate of Naturalization (original), DS-11 application form, passport photo, and payment.

user_message: Can I apply for a green card if I entered the US illegally?
assistant_response: Entering the US illegally (without inspection) generally bars you from adjusting status inside the US, even if you marry a US citizen. You would typically need to leave the US and apply for an immigrant visa at a consulate — but leaving may trigger a 3 or 10-year bar. If you were inspected and admitted (on any visa, even if it expired), you may be eligible to adjust status. Consult an immigration attorney because INA 245(i) and specific family relationships can create exceptions.

user_message: What is an Employment Authorization Document (EAD)?
assistant_response: An EAD (Employment Authorization Document), also called a work permit, is a card issued by USCIS that proves you are authorized to work in the US. It is required for people who are not US citizens or permanent residents but have work authorization — such as asylum seekers, DACA recipients, spouses of certain visa holders (H-4, L-2), adjustment of status applicants, and TPS holders. Apply using Form I-765. Processing takes 3–5 months; you can request expedite for urgent situations.

user_message: What is the difference between H-1B and O-1 visas?
assistant_response: The H-1B visa is for specialty occupation workers with at least a bachelor's degree in a specific field. It is subject to an annual cap (85,000 per year) and requires a lottery if oversubscribed. The O-1 visa is for individuals with extraordinary ability or achievement in sciences, arts, education, business, or athletics. There is no cap or lottery for O-1. O-1 is harder to qualify for (you must prove you are among the top in your field) but can be a great alternative if you do not win the H-1B lottery.

user_message: How do I bring my sibling to the US as a green card holder?
assistant_response: Only US citizens can petition for siblings — green card holders cannot. If you are a US citizen, file Form I-130 for your sibling under the F4 preference category. Because this category has annual limits and high demand, the wait can be 10–20+ years depending on your sibling's country of birth. Countries like Philippines, India, China, and Mexico have the longest backlogs. Your sibling must wait until the Visa Bulletin shows their priority date is current before they can apply for an immigrant visa.

user_message: What is CBP and what do they do at the border?
assistant_response: CBP stands for US Customs and Border Protection, a component of the Department of Homeland Security. At ports of entry (airports, land borders, seaports), CBP officers inspect travelers and cargo entering the US. They verify travel documents, determine admissibility, set your authorized period of stay on your I-94, and can deny admission to the US even if you have a valid visa. A visa grants permission to travel to a port of entry — CBP makes the final admissibility decision.

user_message: What documents should immigrants always carry?
assistant_response: Immigrants should keep these documents safe and accessible: your original passport, visa, I-94 printout, green card or EAD if applicable, I-797 approval notices for any petitions, Social Security card, any court orders or immigration attorney contact information. Carry certified copies for daily use and store originals in a fireproof safe. Never carry documents you do not have originals of. If stopped by immigration enforcement, you have the right to remain silent and speak with an attorney.

user_message: What is the public charge rule and how does it affect my green card application?
assistant_response: The public charge rule allows USCIS to deny a green card to someone deemed likely to become primarily dependent on government benefits. The current rule (as of 2023) considers your age, health, family status, assets, resources, financial status, and education/skills. Receipt of certain cash benefits (SSI, TANF) or long-term institutionalized care can be negative factors. Most immigrants are not affected. Certain categories are exempt, including refugees, asylees, VAWA self-petitioners, and special immigrant juveniles.

user_message: What is an immigration attorney and when do I need one?
assistant_response: An immigration attorney is a licensed lawyer specializing in US immigration law. You should consider one if you have a criminal record, have been previously deported or removed, overstayed a visa, entered without inspection, have a complicated family or work situation, received an RFE or denial, are in removal proceedings, or your case involves waivers. USCIS forms can be filed without an attorney, but mistakes can be costly. Free or low-cost legal help is available through accredited representatives at nonprofit organizations. Never use unauthorized 'notarios.'

user_message: How does the US immigration court system work?
assistant_response: If USCIS issues a Notice to Appear (NTA), you are placed in removal proceedings before an immigration judge in the Executive Office for Immigration Review (EOIR). You have the right to an attorney (at your own expense), the right to present evidence, and the right to appeal. The judge decides whether you should be removed or can stay (through asylum, cancellation of removal, or other relief). Appeals go to the Board of Immigration Appeals (BIA), then to federal circuit courts. The process can take years.

user_message: What is advance parole and when do I need it?
assistant_response: Advance Parole (AP) is a document that allows certain non-citizens (typically those with a pending I-485 adjustment of status, DACA recipients, or TPS holders) to re-enter the US after traveling abroad. Without AP, leaving the US while your I-485 is pending can be considered an abandonment of your application. Apply for AP using Form I-131 — file it simultaneously with your I-485 to minimize delays. AP does not guarantee re-entry; a CBP officer still makes the final admissibility decision.

user_message: What is the difference between removal and deportation?
assistant_response: Removal and deportation mean essentially the same thing — a formal order requiring a foreign national to leave the United States. 'Removal' is the current legal term; 'deportation' is the older term. A removal order can result from overstaying a visa, criminal convictions, immigration fraud, working without authorization, or entering without inspection. Expedited removal applies to those caught near the border recently. A standard removal order goes through immigration court. Being removed can result in a 5-year, 10-year, or permanent bar from re-entering the US.

user_message: Can I get a green card through my job?
assistant_response: Yes, employment-based (EB) green cards are available in five preference categories: EB-1 for priority workers (extraordinary ability, outstanding professors/researchers, multinational managers); EB-2 for advanced degree professionals or exceptional ability; EB-3 for skilled workers and professionals; EB-4 for special immigrants; EB-5 for investors. Most require an employer sponsor and PERM labor certification. EB-1A (extraordinary ability) and EB-2 NIW (national interest waiver) allow self-petitioning. Wait times vary greatly by country of birth.

user_message: What is PERM labor certification?
assistant_response: PERM (Program Electronic Review Management) is the process employers must complete before sponsoring most foreign workers for an employment-based green card (EB-2, EB-3). The employer must conduct good-faith recruitment to show no qualified US workers are available for the position, pay the prevailing wage, and certify these facts to the Department of Labor. PERM approval does not give work authorization — it just clears the way for the employer to file an I-140 petition. PERM applications take 6–18 months; audits can extend that significantly.

user_message: What is an L-1 visa?
assistant_response: The L-1 visa allows multinational companies to transfer employees from foreign offices to US offices. L-1A is for managers and executives (valid up to 7 years); L-1B is for employees with specialized knowledge (valid up to 5 years). There is no annual cap or lottery, and L-1 visa holders can often transition to EB-1C green cards. Requirements: you must have worked for the company abroad for at least 1 continuous year within the past 3 years. The US company must be a parent, subsidiary, affiliate, or branch of the foreign company.

user_message: What is the J-1 exchange visitor visa?
assistant_response: The J-1 visa is for exchange visitors participating in approved programs — researchers, professors, students, teachers, camp counselors, au pairs, medical graduates, interns, and trainees. Many J-1 holders are subject to a 2-year home country physical presence requirement (INA 212(e)), meaning they must return home for 2 years before getting an H or L visa or a green card. You may be able to get a waiver of this requirement through a no-objection statement from your home country, a US government agency, hardship, or persecution.

user_message: What is an EB-5 investor visa?
assistant_response: The EB-5 Immigrant Investor Program allows foreign nationals to get a green card by investing in a US commercial enterprise that creates jobs for US workers. The minimum investment is $1.05 million (or $800,000 in Targeted Employment Areas — rural or high-unemployment areas). You must create or preserve at least 10 full-time jobs for qualifying US workers. Most investors invest through USCIS-designated Regional Centers. Processing can take several years; investors from China and India face long backlogs.

user_message: How do I find free immigration legal help?
assistant_response: Free or low-cost immigration legal help is available through: USCIS-accredited representatives at nonprofit organizations, legal aid societies, law school immigration clinics, Catholic Charities, the International Rescue Committee, the American Immigration Lawyers Association (AILA) pro bono programs, and local bar association referral services. Search online for 'BIA accredited representatives near me' or visit the USCIS website's list of free and low-cost legal services. Avoid notarios and unauthorized immigration consultants — they can take your money and ruin your case.

user_message: What is a Notice to Appear (NTA)?
assistant_response: A Notice to Appear (NTA) is a charging document issued by DHS that places someone in removal proceedings before an immigration judge. It lists the factual allegations and the legal grounds for removal. Receiving an NTA does not mean you will be deported — you have the right to a hearing, to present evidence, and to apply for relief. The NTA specifies your hearing date and location. You should immediately contact an immigration attorney if you receive an NTA. Failing to appear for your hearing results in an automatic removal order.

user_message: What is an I-140 petition?
assistant_response: Form I-140 (Immigrant Petition for Alien Workers) is filed by an employer to sponsor a foreign worker for an employment-based immigrant visa. It establishes that the worker qualifies for the requested preference category and that the employer has the ability to pay the offered wage. After I-140 approval, the worker must wait for a visa number to become available (check the Visa Bulletin) before filing Form I-485 or applying for an immigrant visa abroad. Some I-140 approvals can be ported to new employers under AC21 after 180 days.

user_message: What rights do immigrants have if arrested by ICE?
assistant_response: All people in the US, regardless of immigration status, have constitutional rights. You have the right to remain silent — you do not have to answer questions about your immigration status, birthplace, or how you entered the US. You have the right to speak with an attorney. You have the right to refuse consent to a search of your home without a warrant signed by a judge (not an ICE administrative warrant). Do not sign any documents without speaking with an attorney. Write down the officer's badge number and contact a lawyer as soon as possible.

user_message: What is a conditional green card and how do I remove the conditions?
assistant_response: If you obtain a green card through marriage and the marriage is less than 2 years old when the green card is granted, you receive a conditional green card valid for 2 years. To remove the conditions and get a 10-year green card, you must file Form I-751 (Petition to Remove Conditions on Residence) within the 90-day window before your card expires. Typically you file jointly with your US citizen spouse. If divorced, abused, or widowed, you may file a waiver. USCIS will interview you to verify the marriage is bona fide.

user_message: What is the process for applying for political asylum in the US?
assistant_response: To apply for asylum you must be physically present in the US or at a port of entry and apply within 1 year of arriving (with limited exceptions). File Form I-589 with USCIS for affirmative asylum (if not in removal proceedings) or submit it to the immigration court if in proceedings. You must prove a well-founded fear of persecution based on race, religion, nationality, political opinion, or membership in a particular social group. USCIS or an immigration judge will interview you. Processing can take months to years. If approved, you can apply for a green card after 1 year.

user_message: Can I bring my children to the US on my green card application?
assistant_response: Yes. When you file for a green card, you can include your unmarried children under age 21 as derivatives on your application. File Form I-485 for each derivative child. If your I-130 or I-140 is approved for you as the principal applicant, your derivative children can immigrate with you or follow-to-join within a reasonable time. The Child Status Protection Act (CSPA) can protect children who turn 21 while waiting, keeping them eligible under the original filing date in many cases.

user_message: What is the difference between a US Embassy and a US Consulate?
assistant_response: A US Embassy is the main diplomatic mission of the United States in a foreign country, typically located in the capital city. A US Consulate is a subordinate office located in other major cities of the country. Both can process nonimmigrant and immigrant visas. The Embassy usually handles the most complex visa cases and also handles diplomatic relations. For visa applications, you can often choose which consulate is most convenient — check the US Embassy website for that country to see which locations offer visa appointments.

user_message: What is a biometrics appointment for immigration?
assistant_response: A biometrics appointment is a required step for many USCIS applications where you provide fingerprints, photograph, and signature at an Application Support Center (ASC). USCIS uses this to conduct background checks through the FBI and other agencies. You will receive an appointment notice (Form I-797C) after filing your application. Bring the appointment notice and a valid government-issued photo ID. The appointment takes about 30 minutes. Failing to attend can result in denial of your application. You can reschedule if needed but do so promptly.

user_message: What does 'status violation' mean in immigration?
assistant_response: A status violation occurs when you fail to comply with the conditions of your visa. Examples include: working on a visa that does not allow employment, studying full-time on a B-2 tourist visa, letting your authorized stay expire without extending or departing, or engaging in activities not permitted by your visa type. Status violations can make you ineligible for future visas, trigger removal proceedings, and bar you from adjusting status inside the US. If you realize you have violated status, consult an immigration attorney about options to regularize your situation.

user_message: What is the Green Card Lottery (DV Lottery)?
assistant_response: The Diversity Visa (DV) Lottery, also called the Green Card Lottery, is a US government program that issues up to 55,000 immigrant visas per year to people from countries with historically low immigration rates to the US. Countries with more than 50,000 immigrants in the past 5 years are not eligible (including Mexico, China, India, Philippines, South Korea, UK, Canada). Requirements include a high school diploma or equivalent and 2 years of qualifying work experience. Apply online in October/November each year. Winners are selected randomly.

user_message: What is I-9 employment eligibility verification?
assistant_response: Form I-9 is required for all employees hired in the US. Employers must verify that each employee is legally authorized to work. Employees must provide documents from the I-9 Lists of Acceptable Documents — List A (documents proving both identity and work authorization, like a US passport or green card) OR one document from List B (identity) and one from List C (work authorization). Employers must complete I-9 within 3 business days of hire. Employers cannot request specific documents or discriminate based on citizenship status or national origin.

user_message: Can I get a driver's license as an undocumented immigrant?
assistant_response: It depends on your state. Several states issue driver's licenses or ID cards to undocumented immigrants regardless of legal status. These include California, Colorado, Connecticut, Delaware, Hawaii, Illinois, Maryland, Massachusetts, Minnesota, Nevada, New Jersey, New Mexico, New York, Oregon, Utah, Vermont, Virginia, and Washington. These are sometimes called 'REAL ID non-compliant' licenses, which means they cannot be used for federal purposes like boarding domestic flights. Check your specific state's DMV requirements.

user_message: What is the difference between deportation and voluntary departure?
assistant_response: A formal removal order (deportation) is a government-initiated process that creates a record and can bar you from re-entering for 5–20 years or permanently, depending on circumstances. Voluntary departure allows you to leave at your own expense by a specified date, often without a formal removal order on your record. Voluntary departure may allow you to return sooner. However, if you fail to depart by the deadline, you become subject to a 10-year bar and cannot reopen your immigration case. Consult an attorney to weigh these options.

user_message: How do I apply for a Social Security Number as an immigrant?
assistant_response: If you are authorized to work in the US, you can apply for a Social Security Number (SSN) at a Social Security Administration (SSA) office. Bring original documents proving your immigration status and work authorization (such as your visa, I-94, passport, and EAD if applicable), proof of age (birth certificate), and proof of identity. You can request an SSN on the immigrant visa application (Form DS-260) and SSA will mail it to you after arrival. Nonimmigrant workers (H-1B, L-1, etc.) should wait 10 days after arrival before applying.

user_message: What is an immigration hold or detainer?
assistant_response: An immigration detainer (Form I-247A) is a request from ICE to a local jail or prison to hold a person for up to 48 hours (excluding weekends and holidays) after their criminal case concludes, to allow ICE time to take custody. Detainers are requests — local jurisdictions can choose to honor or ignore them. Several cities and counties are 'sanctuary' jurisdictions that limit cooperation with ICE detainers. If you or a family member is held on an immigration detainer, contact an immigration attorney immediately.

user_message: What is naturalization and who is eligible?
assistant_response: Naturalization is the process by which a non-US citizen becomes a US citizen. To be eligible you must be 18 or older, a lawful permanent resident for 5 years (3 years if married to a US citizen), have continuous residence in the US, have been physically present for at least 30 months of the last 5 years, demonstrate good moral character, pass an English and civics test, and take the Oath of Allegiance. Some exceptions exist for children, military members, and those with physical or mental disabilities. File Form N-400 to begin the process.

user_message: What is an affidavit of support and who needs to file it?
assistant_response: Form I-864 (Affidavit of Support) is a legally binding contract in which a US citizen or green card holder (the sponsor) agrees to financially support an immigrant and ensure they do not become a public charge. It is required for most family-based and some employment-based green card applications. The sponsor must have income at least 125% of the Federal Poverty Guidelines for their household size. The obligation lasts until the immigrant becomes a US citizen, works 40 quarters, leaves the US permanently, or dies.

user_message: What is the Child Status Protection Act (CSPA)?
assistant_response: The Child Status Protection Act (CSPA) protects immigrant children who turn 21 while waiting for their visa application to be processed. Under CSPA, a child's age is calculated as their biological age minus the number of days the visa petition was pending. If the calculated age is under 21, the child retains 'child' classification and does not 'age out.' CSPA also allows children who age out of derivative status to self-petition or port to certain categories. CSPA calculations are complex — consult an immigration attorney if your child is approaching 21.

user_message: What is the difference between an immigrant and a refugee?
assistant_response: An immigrant voluntarily chooses to leave their home country and move to another country, typically for economic, educational, or family reasons. A refugee is forced to flee their home country due to war, violence, or persecution based on race, religion, nationality, political opinion, or membership in a particular social group. Refugees are recognized under the 1951 Refugee Convention. In the US, refugees are admitted through the US Refugee Admissions Program (USRAP), with annual caps set by the President. Both can eventually apply for permanent residency and citizenship.

user_message: How do I report immigration fraud or a notario?
assistant_response: Immigration fraud — where unauthorized practitioners (notarios, consultants) charge fees for legal immigration services they are not authorized to provide — is a serious problem. To report: file a complaint with your state attorney general's consumer protection office, the Federal Trade Commission at ftc.gov/complaint, your state bar association (if someone claimed to be an attorney but is not), and the USCIS tip line at 1-800-375-5283. If you paid a notario and got incorrect documents filed, an accredited representative or attorney may be able to help you reopen your case.

user_message: What is the process for an H-1B visa lottery?
assistant_response: The H-1B cap lottery runs each April for an October 1 start date. Your employer must register you in the USCIS electronic registration system in March (registration fee: $215 per beneficiary). If selected in the lottery, your employer then has 90 days to file the full H-1B petition. There are 65,000 visas for the regular cap and 20,000 additional visas for workers with a US master's degree or higher. Cap-exempt employers (universities, nonprofits affiliated with universities, certain government research organizations) can file any time.

user_message: What documents prove a bona fide marriage for immigration purposes?
assistant_response: To prove your marriage is genuine (not for immigration purposes), gather: joint bank account statements, joint lease or mortgage documents, joint tax returns (Form 1040), insurance policies listing each other as beneficiaries, car registration showing the same address, utility bills in both names, photos of the couple together over time, correspondence between you and your spouse, affidavits from friends and family who know you as a couple, and children's birth certificates if applicable. The more documentation, the stronger your case. USCIS officers look for evidence of a real shared life.

user_message: Can I work for any employer on an H-1B visa?
assistant_response: No. H-1B status is employer-specific — you are authorized to work only for the petitioning employer in the specific position and location listed on the petition. If you change jobs or your employer significantly modifies your role, title, or work location, a new or amended H-1B petition must be filed. You can work for multiple employers simultaneously only if each files a separate H-1B petition for you. If you lose your H-1B job, you have a 60-day grace period to find a new employer, change status, or depart the US.

user_message: What is a US visa stamp and how is it different from status?
assistant_response: A visa stamp is a sticker placed in your passport by a US consulate that allows you to travel to a US port of entry and request admission. It has an expiration date showing when you must travel by. Your immigration status is determined by the I-94 issued at the port of entry — it shows your visa type and authorized period of stay. You can remain in the US with valid status even if your visa stamp expires, as long as your I-94 is valid. You only need a valid visa stamp when traveling internationally and re-entering the US.

user_message: What are sanctuary cities?
assistant_response: Sanctuary cities are jurisdictions (cities, counties, or states) that limit cooperation between local law enforcement and federal immigration enforcement (ICE). Policies vary by location but typically include not asking about immigration status during routine policing, not holding people in jail solely based on ICE detainers, and not sharing information about individuals with ICE without a court order. Sanctuary policies are intended to encourage immigrant community members to report crimes and cooperate with police without fear of deportation. These are local policies — federal immigration law still applies everywhere.

user_message: What is the Violence Against Women Act (VAWA) and how does it help immigrants?
assistant_response: VAWA allows certain immigrants who have been abused by a US citizen or lawful permanent resident spouse, parent, or adult child to self-petition for a green card without the abuser's knowledge or cooperation. The abused immigrant files Form I-360 confidentially. VAWA protects women, men, and children. Approval allows the petitioner to apply for a green card. There are also VAWA protections in immigration court proceedings. Hotline for domestic violence: 1-800-799-7233 (National Domestic Violence Hotline). These cases are handled with strict confidentiality.

user_message: What is an O-1 visa and who qualifies?
assistant_response: The O-1 visa is for individuals with extraordinary ability in science, arts, education, business, or athletics (O-1A) or extraordinary achievement in motion picture or television (O-1B). You must demonstrate sustained national or international acclaim through awards, media coverage, high salary compared to peers, critical roles in distinguished organizations, original contributions, or judging others' work. There is no cap or lottery. O-1 visas are initially valid up to 3 years with 1-year extensions. A US employer or agent must petition on your behalf using Form I-129.

user_message: What is the EB-1A extraordinary ability green card?
assistant_response: EB-1A allows people with extraordinary ability in science, arts, education, business, or athletics to self-petition for a green card without an employer sponsor. You must meet at least 3 of 10 criteria: major prizes/awards, membership in elite associations, published material about you, judging others' work, original contributions, authorship of scholarly articles, display of artistic work, leading roles in distinguished organizations, high salary, or commercial success. You must be coming to continue work in your field. No PERM required. Processing is typically faster than other employment categories.

user_message: How long does USCIS take to process applications?
assistant_response: Processing times vary widely by form type, service center, and current caseload. Check current processing times at egov.uscis.gov/processing-times. As rough guides: I-130 (family petition) 12–24+ months; I-485 (green card adjustment) 8–24 months; I-765 (EAD/work permit) 3–5 months; N-400 (naturalization) 8–21 months; I-539 (change of status) 6–12 months; H-1B petition 3–6 months (15 business days with premium). Times fluctuate significantly. You can submit an inquiry (e-request) if your case exceeds the published processing time.

user_message: What is the Visa Waiver Program (VWP)?
assistant_response: The Visa Waiver Program (VWP) allows citizens of 42 countries to travel to the US for tourism or business for up to 90 days without a visa, using ESTA (Electronic System for Travel Authorization). VWP countries include most of Western Europe, Japan, South Korea, Australia, and New Zealand. You must apply for ESTA at esta.cbp.dhs.gov before travel ($21 fee). ESTA approval is usually instant. If your ESTA is denied, you must apply for a B-1/B-2 visa. VWP travelers cannot extend their stay and have limited appeal rights if denied entry.

user_message: What is an EB-2 NIW (National Interest Waiver)?
assistant_response: EB-2 NIW (National Interest Waiver) allows foreign nationals with advanced degrees or exceptional ability to self-petition for a green card without an employer sponsor or PERM labor certification. You must show your work is in an area of substantial intrinsic merit, your work has national scope, and the benefits of waiving the job offer requirement outweigh the labor market protections. This is often used by researchers, scientists, doctors, and entrepreneurs. The Dhanasar standard (2016 BIA precedent decision) guides EB-2 NIW adjudications. No cap specifically applies, but wait times vary by country.

user_message: Can I open a bank account as an immigrant without a Social Security Number?
assistant_response: Yes. Many banks and credit unions allow immigrants to open accounts without a Social Security Number using an Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN) or, in some cases, just a foreign passport and visa. Banks like Bank of America, Chase, and Wells Fargo have programs for this. Some banks accept Mexican Matricula Consular cards. Credit unions often have more flexible policies. You can apply for an ITIN (Form W-7) from the IRS if you need to file taxes but are not eligible for an SSN. An ITIN does not authorize employment.

user_message: What is a departure bar in immigration?
assistant_response: A departure bar is a rule that prevents certain immigrants from filing or continuing certain immigration applications after leaving the US. For example, if you have a pending I-485 adjustment of status and you leave the US without Advance Parole, your I-485 may be deemed abandoned. Similarly, if you are in removal proceedings, departing the US can complicate or end your case. Some waivers require that you file before departing. Always check with an immigration attorney before traveling internationally if you have any pending immigration applications.

user_message: What healthcare options do immigrants have in the US?
assistant_response: Healthcare options depend on your immigration status. Lawful permanent residents (after 5 years) and citizens can access Medicaid and CHIP in most states. Some states provide Medicaid to recent immigrants regardless of the 5-year bar. Undocumented immigrants are generally not eligible for federal programs but may access emergency Medicaid. All immigrants can purchase insurance through the ACA marketplace. Community health centers (federally qualified health centers) provide care on a sliding fee scale regardless of status. Children under 18 may qualify for CHIP in many states regardless of immigration status.

user_message: What is the process if ICE comes to my home?
assistant_response: If ICE agents come to your home: Do not open the door unless they have a warrant signed by a judge (an administrative ICE warrant is not the same as a judicial warrant). Ask them to slide the warrant under the door — if it is signed by a federal judge and says 'search warrant,' they can enter. If it is only signed by an ICE officer, you do not have to open the door. You have the right to remain silent. Do not run. Do not physically resist. Say clearly: 'I am exercising my right to remain silent and I want to speak to a lawyer.' Contact an immigration attorney immediately.

user_message: How do immigrant children access public schools?
assistant_response: All children in the US, regardless of immigration status, have the right to a free public K-12 education under the Supreme Court ruling in Plyler v. Doe (1982). Schools cannot ask about immigration status or require a Social Security Number as a condition of enrollment. Typical enrollment documents include proof of age (birth certificate), proof of residency (utility bill, lease), and immunization records. Schools must also provide English language learners with appropriate language instruction. This right applies to undocumented children as well.

user_message: What is a USCIS case status and how do I check it?
assistant_response: You can check your USCIS case status online at egov.uscis.gov/casestatus using your receipt number from your I-797 notice of action. Your receipt number is a 13-character code starting with letters (e.g., EAC, WAC, LIN, SRC, NBC, MSC, IOE). Status updates include 'Received,' 'Biometrics Appointment Scheduled,' 'Interview Scheduled,' 'Request for Evidence,' 'Approved,' 'Card Was Mailed,' etc. You can also sign up for email/text notifications. If your case has been pending beyond the published processing time, you can submit an e-request through the USCIS website.

user_message: What is an EAD combo card?
assistant_response: A combo card (combination card) is a single card that serves as both an Employment Authorization Document (EAD) and an Advance Parole (AP) travel document. It is issued to adjustment of status (I-485) applicants who apply for both Form I-765 (work authorization) and Form I-131 (travel document) at the same time. The combo card has the class 'C11' on it. It allows you to work in the US and travel internationally and re-enter without abandoning your I-485, as long as you meet admissibility requirements. USCIS now routinely issues these as combo cards.

user_message: What is the TN visa for Canadians and Mexicans?
assistant_response: The TN (Trade NAFTA/USMCA) visa allows Canadian and Mexican citizens to work in the US in specific professional categories listed in the USMCA agreement, such as accountants, engineers, lawyers, scientists, pharmacists, and computer systems analysts. Canadian citizens can apply at the US port of entry (no advance petition needed). Mexican citizens must apply at a US consulate. TN status is granted in 3-year increments with unlimited renewals. TN holders cannot self-petition for a green card — a separate immigrant petition is needed.

user_message: How do I appeal a USCIS denial?
assistant_response: After a USCIS denial, you have several options depending on the form type: (1) File a Motion to Reopen or Motion to Reconsider (Form I-290B) with USCIS — a motion to reopen presents new facts; a motion to reconsider argues USCIS made a legal error. The filing fee is $810. (2) Appeal to the Administrative Appeals Office (AAO) for certain case types using Form I-290B. (3) If in removal proceedings, the immigration judge and Board of Immigration Appeals can hear the case. (4) File a federal court lawsuit (mandamus or APA) in certain situations. Deadlines are strict — consult an attorney quickly.

user_message: What is an E-2 treaty investor visa?
assistant_response: The E-2 treaty investor visa allows nationals of certain treaty countries to enter the US to direct and develop a business in which they have invested or are actively investing a substantial amount of capital. Requirements: you must be a national of a treaty country, the investment must be real and active (not speculative), it must be at least 50% owned by treaty-country nationals, and you must be in a directing or essential role. There is no minimum investment amount, but it must be substantial relative to the business. E-2 does not lead directly to a green card.

user_message: What are the consequences of immigration fraud?
assistant_response: Immigration fraud — including misrepresentation on applications, sham marriages, false claims to US citizenship, or document fraud — carries severe consequences: permanent inadmissibility bar to the US (INA 212(a)(6)(C)), criminal prosecution (up to 5–10 years in prison), deportation, and bars on future immigration benefits. USCIS and CBP officers are trained to detect fraud. If you made a misrepresentation, consult an immigration attorney immediately about whether a waiver (Form I-601) is available. Voluntary disclosure before discovery may provide some protection.

user_message: What is expedite request and when can I request it from USCIS?
assistant_response: USCIS may grant expedited processing in limited circumstances: (1) Severe financial loss to a company or individual; (2) Urgent humanitarian reasons; (3) US military deployment; (4) Congressional interest; (5) USCIS error that caused a delay; (6) Compelling need supported by credible evidence. Submit your expedite request by calling the USCIS Contact Center (1-800-375-5283) or through the online account. Include documentation supporting your reason. Expedite approvals are discretionary and not guaranteed. Routine processing delays or desired travel are generally not qualifying reasons.

user_message: What is a U visa for crime victims?
assistant_response: The U visa is available to victims of certain qualifying crimes (including domestic violence, sexual assault, kidnapping, manslaughter, and others) who have suffered substantial abuse, possess information about the crime, and have been helpful, are being helpful, or are likely to be helpful to law enforcement in the detection, investigation, or prosecution of the crime. A law enforcement agency must sign Form I-918B certifying the victim's cooperation. U visa holders can legally work in the US and apply for a green card after 3 years in U status. There are 10,000 U visas per year with a long waitlist.

user_message: What is a T visa for trafficking victims?
assistant_response: The T visa is for victims of severe forms of human trafficking — including sex trafficking and labor trafficking. To qualify you must be a victim of trafficking, be physically present in the US due to trafficking, comply with any reasonable law enforcement requests (exceptions for trauma), and demonstrate you would suffer extreme hardship if removed. T visa holders can work legally and after 3 years (or end of prosecution, whichever comes first) can apply for a green card. Up to 5,000 T visas are available per year. Family members may also be eligible.

user_message: What is humanitarian parole?
assistant_response: Humanitarian parole allows USCIS to grant temporary entry to the US to individuals who are otherwise inadmissible, for urgent humanitarian reasons or significant public benefit. It is discretionary and granted on a case-by-case basis. Parole does not confer immigration status and cannot lead directly to a green card (except in specific programs). Recent uses include the Uniting for Ukraine program, Cuban, Haitian, Nicaraguan, and Venezuelan parole programs (CHNV). Parolees can apply for work authorization and after 1 year may apply for asylum. File Form I-131 for advance parole or I-131A in certain programs.

user_message: How do I handle a request for evidence (RFE) for my H-1B?
assistant_response: When you receive an H-1B RFE, read it carefully to identify every issue raised. Common H-1B RFEs question whether the position qualifies as a specialty occupation, whether your degree is in a directly related field, or whether the employer-employee relationship is real (especially for IT consulting placements). Respond within the deadline (87 days). Include the original RFE with your response. Address every point with specific evidence: detailed job description, expert opinion letters, educational credential evaluations, end-client letters for consulting positions, and legal briefs. A well-documented response significantly improves your chances.

user_message: What is a visa overstay and how does USCIS know?
assistant_response: A visa overstay occurs when you remain in the US beyond the date shown on your I-94. DHS tracks overstays through biographic information matched against departure records from airlines, CBP at land borders, and the I-94 system. Since 2016 DHS publishes annual overstay reports. If you overstay 180+ days, a 3-year bar kicks in upon departure; 1+ year overstay triggers a 10-year bar. These bars are permanent for the covered period and require a waiver (Form I-601) to overcome for future entry. Always check your I-94 carefully and leave before the authorized period expires.

user_message: What is the I-551 stamp and why might I need it?
assistant_response: The I-551 stamp (also called an Alien Documentation, Identification and Telecommunications stamp or ADIT stamp) is a temporary evidence of permanent resident status stamped in your passport by USCIS. You may need it if your green card is expired, lost, or damaged and you need to travel or prove work authorization while waiting for a replacement. USCIS or an immigration judge can issue the I-551 stamp. It typically is valid for 1 year. To get one, you usually need to attend an InfoPass appointment (now called USCIS office appointments) and bring your expired or receipt documents.

user_message: What is the Electronic System for Travel Authorization (ESTA)?
assistant_response: ESTA is the online system that determines whether travelers under the Visa Waiver Program (VWP) are eligible to travel to the US. You must complete ESTA at esta.cbp.dhs.gov before travel ($21 fee). ESTA approval is typically instant and is valid for 2 years or until your passport expires, whichever comes first, allowing multiple trips up to 90 days each. You need a new ESTA when you get a new passport. If your ESTA is denied, you are not eligible for VWP and must apply for a B visa. ESTA is not a visa — it is a pre-travel authorization.

user_message: What is the K-3 visa for spouses of US citizens?
assistant_response: The K-3 visa was created to allow foreign spouses of US citizens to enter the US while their I-130 immigrant petition was being processed, reducing family separation. However, because USCIS now processes I-130 petitions faster than K-3 petitions in many cases, K-3 is rarely used. The K-3 holder can apply for work authorization and must apply for adjustment of status (I-485) to get a green card. If your I-130 is approved before your K-3 visa appointment, you should apply for an immigrant visa directly. Consult an immigration attorney on which path is faster in your specific case.

user_message: What is a removal order and what happens after?
assistant_response: A removal order is a legal order requiring a person to leave the United States. It can be issued by an immigration judge after a hearing or, in expedited cases, by a CBP or ICE officer at the border. After a removal order is issued, ICE may detain and deport the person. A removal order may result in a 5-year, 10-year, or permanent bar from re-entering the US, depending on the circumstances. You can appeal to the BIA and then to a federal circuit court. Some people may qualify for a stay of removal (temporary halt) while appeals are pending.

user_message: What is the USCIS fee waiver and who qualifies?
assistant_response: USCIS offers fee waivers (Form I-912) for certain immigration applications for people who cannot afford to pay. You may qualify if your household income is at or below 150% of the Federal Poverty Guidelines, you receive means-tested public benefits, or you can demonstrate financial hardship. Not all forms are eligible for fee waivers — common ones that are include I-485, I-765, I-131, I-90, and N-400. Provide documentation of income (tax returns, pay stubs, benefit letters). Fee waivers are adjudicated by USCIS and are not guaranteed.

user_message: What is a humanitarian visa?
assistant_response: Humanitarian visas are various immigration benefits available to people with urgent humanitarian needs. These include: asylum (for those fearing persecution), refugee status, withholding of removal, Convention Against Torture (CAT) protection, U visas (crime victims), T visas (trafficking victims), VAWA self-petitions (domestic violence victims), Special Immigrant Juvenile Status (abused/neglected/abandoned children), humanitarian parole, and Temporary Protected Status. Each has different eligibility criteria and benefits. Many are processed through USCIS; others through the immigration court system. Consult an immigration attorney or accredited representative.

user_message: What is Special Immigrant Juvenile Status (SIJS)?
assistant_response: Special Immigrant Juvenile Status (SIJS) is an immigration classification for children in the US who have been abused, neglected, or abandoned by one or both parents. A state juvenile court must first make specific findings (that reunification with one or both parents is not viable due to abuse, neglect, or abandonment, and that it is not in the child's best interest to be returned to their home country). With those findings, the child can file Form I-360 for SIJS and then apply for a green card. SIJS is available until the child turns 21 (with CSPA protections in some circuits).

user_message: How do I check if an immigration attorney is legitimate?
assistant_response: To verify an immigration attorney: (1) Check their bar number with the state bar association — every attorney must be licensed in at least one US state. (2) Search the AILA (American Immigration Lawyers Association) member directory at aila.org. (3) Check for disciplinary history on the state bar website. (4) Verify they are not on the EOIR list of disciplined practitioners. (5) Get a written fee agreement before paying anything. (6) Never pay cash without a receipt. Avoid 'notarios,' 'immigration consultants,' or 'visa agents' who are not licensed attorneys or BIA-accredited representatives — they cannot legally provide immigration legal advice.

user_message: What is the I-751 petition to remove conditions?
assistant_response: If you got a green card through marriage and the marriage was less than 2 years old at the time, you received a 2-year conditional green card. To get a permanent 10-year green card, you must file Form I-751 (Petition to Remove Conditions on Residence) jointly with your US citizen or permanent resident spouse within the 90-day window before your conditional card expires. You must prove the marriage was bona fide. If you divorced, were abused, or your spouse died, you can file a waiver. If approved, you get a 10-year green card. Late filings may still be accepted with a good excuse.

user_message: What language rights do immigrants have in the US?
assistant_response: Federal law requires that entities receiving federal funds provide meaningful access to people with limited English proficiency (LEP) — this includes courts, hospitals, social service agencies, and schools. You have the right to a court interpreter in immigration proceedings. You have the right to receive vital documents in your language from federally funded agencies. You have the right to an interpreter when applying for federal benefits. Many states have additional language access laws. Under Title VI of the Civil Rights Act, discrimination based on national origin (which includes language) is prohibited in federally funded programs.
